# Siloed mentality, health system suboptimization and the healthcare symphony: a Canadian perspective

[*Health Research Policy and Systems*](/)
**volume 22**, Article number: 87 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

3632 Accesses

12 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w/metrics)

## Abstract

Measuring and optimizing a health system is challenging when patient care is split between many independent organizations. For example, patients receive care from their primary care provider, outpatient specialist clinics, hospitals, private providers and, in some instances, family members. These silos are maintained through different funding sources (or lack of funding) which incentivize siloed service delivery. A shift towards prioritizing patient outcomes and keeping the patient at the centre of care is emerging. However, competing philosophies on patient needs, how health is defined and how health is produced and funded is creating and engraining silos in the delivery of health services. Healthcare and health outcomes are produced through a series of activities conducted by diverse teams of health professionals working in concert. Health professionals are continually learning from each patient interaction; however, silos are barriers to information exchange, collaborative evidence generation and health system improvement. This paper presents a systems view of healthcare and provides a systems lens to approach current challenges in health systems. The first part of the paper provides a background on the current state and challenges to healthcare in Canada. The second part presents potential reasons for continued health system underperformance. The paper concludes with a system perspective for addressing these challenges.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w/peer-review)

## Introduction

The global pandemic caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) pushed health systems past their capacity limit globally, and exposed existing system inefficiencies. Canadian hospitals have been operating at 100–120% capacity and this continued overloading undermines their ability to deliver safe, effective and timely care [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR1 "HealthCareCAN. COVID-19 amplifies health system capacity shortfalls. 2021. 
                  https://www.healthcarecan.ca/wp-content/themes/camyno/assets/document/PolicyDocs/2021/PolicyBrief-CapacityShortfalls_EN.pdf?target=blank
                  
                .")]. Vaccines lower the risk of getting and spreading SARS-CoV-2 and prevent serious illness and death, yet vaccine hesitancy both during and continuing postpandemic have limited their public health benefit. Delayed or cancelled surgeries were common during the pandemic. Temporary hospital units were created to ease overcrowding and overcapacity, and healthcare workers retired from the workforce, quit or moved to alternate employment, often due to burnout, stress, anxiety and depression in dedicated frontline workers [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR1 "HealthCareCAN. COVID-19 amplifies health system capacity shortfalls. 2021. 
                  https://www.healthcarecan.ca/wp-content/themes/camyno/assets/document/PolicyDocs/2021/PolicyBrief-CapacityShortfalls_EN.pdf?target=blank
                  
                .")]. This led to shortages which exacerbated capacity issues and in a vicious cycle created heavy workloads resulting in more burnout [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR1 "HealthCareCAN. COVID-19 amplifies health system capacity shortfalls. 2021. 
                  https://www.healthcarecan.ca/wp-content/themes/camyno/assets/document/PolicyDocs/2021/PolicyBrief-CapacityShortfalls_EN.pdf?target=blank
                  
                .")]. Four years after the WHO declared a pandemic (11 March 2020), health systems are still struggling. The global pandemic exaggerated existing problematic structures and put health systems into the spotlight. Our health systems have a complex role in promoting, restoring, and maintaining health of people and populations but they are falling short as they face increasing patient demand and pressures to perform.

Performance and cost are related. Health expenditures as a share of gross domestic product (GDP) are trending upwards in Canada [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR2 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. National health expenditure trends, 1975 to 2018. Ottawa: CIHI; 2018.")]. Among the 36 countries that make up the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), in 2023, Canada spent an estimated 12.1% of GDP, or $8740 CAD per capita on healthcare and is among the highest spenders, above the OECD average of 9.7% of GDP or $6044 CAD per capita in 2021 [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR3 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. Health expenditure data in brief. 2023. 
                  https://www.cihi.ca/sites/default/files/document/health-expenditure-data-in-brief-2023-en.pdf
                  
                ."), [4](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR4 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. National health expenditure trends, 2023—Snapshot. 2023. 
                  https://www.cihi.ca/en/national-health-expenditure-trends-2023-snapshot
                  
                .")]. In return, Canada’s healthcare performance ranks second to last; the 2021 Commonwealth Fund report ranked Canada tenth of eleven countries performing poorly in access, equity and healthcare outcome categories – only ahead of the United States [[5](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR5 "Schneider EC, Shah A, Doty MM, Tikkanen R, Fields K, Williams II RD. Mirror, mirror 2021: reflecting poorly—health care in the U.S. compared to other high-income countries. 2021. 
                  https://www.commonwealthfund.org/publications/fund-reports/2021/aug/mirror-mirror-2021-reflecting-poorly
                  
                .")]. Technological and pharmacological advances, increased demand and public expectations, prescription drug expenditures and an ageing population are drivers of increased healthcare spending [[6](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR6 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. Health care cost drivers: the facts. Ottawa: CIHI; 2011.")].

Health systems are complex adaptive systems, where health system performance and behaviours change over time [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR7 "Braithwaite J. Changing how we think about healthcare improvement. Br Med J. 2018;361: k2014.")]. Healthcare settings, people and processes evolve dynamically in terms of relationships and interactions [[8](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR8 "Greenhalgh T, Papoutsi C. Spreading and scaling up innovation and improvement. BMJ. 2019;365: l2068.")]. The nonlinearity of complex adaptive systems results in numerous ways to produce the same health outcomes. Empiric data and evidence are required – the clues to health improvement and population health must be observed from the differences within and between populations over time [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR9 "Rose G. Sick individuals and sick populations. Int J Epidemiol. 1985;14(1):32–8.")].

In this early postpandemic vantage point, we explore the Canadian health system as an exemplar to try to understand the path to better performance and better outcomes at both the individual and the societal level. Our underlying thesis is that health systems are complex adaptive systems whose components are not necessarily additive.

### The problem

The Canadian health system is still not meeting the needs of the populations they serve. It is experiencing physician, nurse and healthcare worker shortages [[10](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR10 "Howlett K. Canada’s supply of family doctors doesn’t match demand, and provinces lack data to find out why. 2022. 
                  https://www.theglobeandmail.com/canada/article-family-doctors-canada-data-deficit/
                  
                .")]; increasing wait times [[11](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR11 "Moir M, Barua B. Waiting your turn: wait times for health care in Canada, 2021 report. Fraser Institute; 2021. 
                  https://www.fraserinstitute.org/sites/default/files/waiting-your-turn-2021-exsum-natl.pdf
                  
                .")]; reduced preventive care service delivery [[12](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR12 "Laing S, Johnston S. Estimated impact of COVID-19 on preventive care service delivery: an observational cohort study. BMC Health Serv Res. 2021;21(1):1107.")] and increasing costs [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR2 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. National health expenditure trends, 1975 to 2018. Ottawa: CIHI; 2018.")]. A fundamental problem is suboptimization, where productivity improvements in one area (silo) can result in system level decreases in productivity [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR13 "Kamarainen VJ, Peltokorpi A, Torkki P, Tallbacka K. Measuring healthcare productivity—from unit to system level. Int J Health Care Qual Assur. 2016;29(3):288–99.")]. A common assumption is that a higher unit level productivity will result in a higher system level productivity [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR13 "Kamarainen VJ, Peltokorpi A, Torkki P, Tallbacka K. Measuring healthcare productivity—from unit to system level. Int J Health Care Qual Assur. 2016;29(3):288–99.")] or that maximizing patient outcomes through disease-specific clinical practice guidelines result in superior patient well-being and outcomes. However, real life observations on patient outcomes [[14](#ref-CR14 "St John PD, Menec V, Tyas SL, Tate R, Griffith L. Multimorbidity in Canadians living in the community: results from the Canadian longitudinal study of aging. Can Fam Physician Medecin de famille canadien. 2021;67(3):187–97."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Geda NR, Janzen B, Pahwa P. Chronic disease multimorbidity among the Canadian population: prevalence and associated lifestyle factors. Arch Public Health. 2021;79(1):60."),[16](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR16 "St John PD, Tyas SL, Menec V, Tate R. Multimorbidity, disability, and mortality in community-dwelling older adults. Can Fam Physician Medecin de famille canadien. 2014;60(5):e272–80.")] and health system performance outcomes [[3](#ref-CR3 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. Health expenditure data in brief. 2023. 
                  https://www.cihi.ca/sites/default/files/document/health-expenditure-data-in-brief-2023-en.pdf
                  
                ."),[4](#ref-CR4 "Canadian Institute for Health Information. National health expenditure trends, 2023—Snapshot. 2023. 
                  https://www.cihi.ca/en/national-health-expenditure-trends-2023-snapshot
                  
                ."),[5](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR5 "Schneider EC, Shah A, Doty MM, Tikkanen R, Fields K, Williams II RD. Mirror, mirror 2021: reflecting poorly—health care in the U.S. compared to other high-income countries. 2021. 
                  https://www.commonwealthfund.org/publications/fund-reports/2021/aug/mirror-mirror-2021-reflecting-poorly
                  
                .")] indicate otherwise. The disconnect between individual patients, practitioners, organizations and system outcomes are due to an unclear link between production and value [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR13 "Kamarainen VJ, Peltokorpi A, Torkki P, Tallbacka K. Measuring healthcare productivity—from unit to system level. Int J Health Care Qual Assur. 2016;29(3):288–99.")].

This discordance arises, in part perhaps due to the priorities which lead to measurement at different levels of the health system. Health system stakeholders have numerous, sometimes conflicting goals around access to services, cost reduction, quality, cost containment, safety, patient centredness and satisfaction [[17](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR17 "Porter ME. What is value in health care? N Engl J Med. 2010;363(26):2477–81.")]. Healthcare units and stakeholders usually define performance outcomes based on discrete patient needs and demands. For example, the health unit level may use ratio-based measures such as labour-hours per patient or care episode and the number of resources consumed. At the organizational level, performance metrics measure hospital productivity, wait times, unit capacity or number of procedures performed. At the system or macro level, performance metrics can be health expenditures as a share of gross domestic product or broadly defined measures such as access, safety and quality of care. Siloed outcomes between units and organizations cannot be efficient from a systems perspective [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR13 "Kamarainen VJ, Peltokorpi A, Torkki P, Tallbacka K. Measuring healthcare productivity—from unit to system level. Int J Health Care Qual Assur. 2016;29(3):288–99.")]. A holistic integrated systems approach to patient care and productivity can align health system stakeholders to provide the best health services from a system perspective [[13](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR13 "Kamarainen VJ, Peltokorpi A, Torkki P, Tallbacka K. Measuring healthcare productivity—from unit to system level. Int J Health Care Qual Assur. 2016;29(3):288–99."), [17](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR17 "Porter ME. What is value in health care? N Engl J Med. 2010;363(26):2477–81.")].

## Health systems

Health systems that were historically devoted to prevention and treatment of infectious diseases and discrete episodes of acute care are now faced with the management of chronic health conditions such as heart disease, diabetes mellitus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and many types of cancer [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR18 "Institute of Medicine (US), editor. Evidence-based medicine and the changing nature of healthcare: 1, the changing nature of health care. 2007 IOM annual meeting summary. Washington (DC): National Academies Press; 2008.")]. In the early eighteenth-century health systems focus on the isolation of the ill and quarantining of the exposed to contain contagious diseases [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR19 "Institute of Medicine (US) Committee for the Study of the Future of Public Health. 3, A history of the public health system. The future of public health. Washington (DC): National Academies Press; 1988.")]. These systems evolved into voluntary general hospitals for the physically ill and institutions for the mentally ill in the late eighteenth century [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR19 "Institute of Medicine (US) Committee for the Study of the Future of Public Health. 3, A history of the public health system. The future of public health. Washington (DC): National Academies Press; 1988.")]. The nineteenth century brought about the importance of sanitation as a mechanism to address the causes of disease and the vehicle for transmission [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR19 "Institute of Medicine (US) Committee for the Study of the Future of Public Health. 3, A history of the public health system. The future of public health. Washington (DC): National Academies Press; 1988.")]. Diseases were considered an “indicator of a societal problems as well as a personal problem” [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR19 "Institute of Medicine (US) Committee for the Study of the Future of Public Health. 3, A history of the public health system. The future of public health. Washington (DC): National Academies Press; 1988.")].

Improvements in technology, medical care and public health policy have resulted in increased survival rates from previously fatal health conditions. Declining infectious disease deaths resulting in longer life expectancies and the demographic trends of larger numbers of births post-second world war and now declining fertility rates globally have resulted in an increasing proportion of older citizens and a greater segment of the population with multiple age-associated concurrent medical conditions (multimorbidity) [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR20 "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs (UNDESA). Chapter 1: An ageing world. In: Luchsinger G, editor. World social report 2023: leaving no one behind in an ageing world. UN2023. p. 17–34."), [21](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR21 "United Nations Department of Economic and Social Affairs (UNDESA). Chapter 5: A crisis of care. In: Luchsinger G, editor. World social report 2023: leaving no one behind in an ageing world. UN; 2023. p. 113–49.")]. These chronic conditions are not so much curable as manageable. This makes multimorbidity both costly and associated with increased health system utilization and poorer health outcomes [[22](#ref-CR22 "Agborsangaya CB, Lau D, Lahtinen M, Cooke T, Johnson JA. Health-related quality of life and healthcare utilization in multimorbidity: results of a cross-sectional survey. Qual Life Res. 2013;22(4):791–9."),[23](#ref-CR23 "Fortin M, Lapointe L, Hudon C, Vanasse A, Ntetu AL, Maltais D. Multimorbidity and quality of life in primary care: a systematic review. Health Qual Life Outcomes. 2004;2:51."),[24](#ref-CR24 "Wolff JL, Starfield B, Anderson G. Prevalence, expenditures, and complications of multiple chronic conditions in the elderly. Arch Intern Med. 2002;162(20):2269–76."),[25](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR25 "Tonelli M, Wiebe N, Straus S, Fortin M, Guthrie B, James MT, et al. Multimorbidity, dementia and health care in older people: a population-based cohort study. CMAJ Open. 2017;5(3):E623–31.")]. Individuals with multimorbidity have increased risks of receiving less than the best practice care [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR26 "Bayliss EA, Edwards AE, Steiner JF, Main DS. Processes of care desired by elderly patients with multimorbidities. Fam Pract. 2008;25(4):287–93."), [27](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR27 "Vogeli C, Shields AE, Lee TA, Gibson TB, Marder WD, Weiss KB, et al. Multiple chronic conditions: prevalence, health consequences, and implications for quality, care management, and costs. J Gen Intern Med. 2007;22:391–5.")], higher healthcare costs, increased polypharmacy, and longer and more frequent hospitalizations [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR28 "Condelius A, Edberg AK, Jakobsson U, Hallberg IR. Hospital admissions among people 65+ related to multimorbidity, municipal and outpatient care. Arch Gerontol Geriat. 2008;46(1):41–55.")]. Significant inefficiencies emerge in health systems, as systems built to cope with single acute illnesses try to cope with new and more complex demands. Excess costs are caused by overuse of unnecessary health services or technologies, inefficiently delivered health services, medical errors, mispriced health technologies and missed prevention opportunities [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR29 "Smith KE. Beyond evidence based policy in public health: the interplay of ideas. Basingstoke: Palgrave Macmillan; 2013.")].

Reductionist approaches to each multimorbid condition can leave patients to navigate a complex health system and encounter fragmentation of care, duplication of services and treatment errors [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR30 "Institute of Medicine (US). Best care at lower cost: The path to continuously learning health care in America. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press; 2013.")]. Coordinating and communicating needs to different providers can be overwhelming, resulting in poor health outcomes [[30](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR30 "Institute of Medicine (US). Best care at lower cost: The path to continuously learning health care in America. Washington, DC: The National Academies Press; 2013.")]. This fragmented, disease-centric care is maintained through clinical practice guidelines oriented towards single diseases and physician incentives, including fee-for-service healthcare professional remuneration linked to discrete International Classification of Disease (ICD) codes [[31](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR31 "Tinetti M, Fried T, Boyd C. Designing health care for the most common chronic condition-multimorbidity. J Am Med Assoc. 2012;307(23):2493–4.")]. The result is that the specialist is responsible for and focuses upon a single disease among a patient’s many chronic conditions and not the whole patient [[31](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR31 "Tinetti M, Fried T, Boyd C. Designing health care for the most common chronic condition-multimorbidity. J Am Med Assoc. 2012;307(23):2493–4.")]. Basing care on disease-specific clinical practice guidelines poses care challenges for patients with multimorbidity and may have unintended effects [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR32 "Boyd CM, Darer J, Boult C, Fried LP, Boult L, Wu AW. Clinical practice guidelines and quality of care for older patients with multiple comorbid diseases—implications for pay for performance. JAMA. 2005;294(6):716–24.")].

The complexity of the interaction between the patient and health system means that healthcare is a complex adaptive system comprising a network of components (stakeholders, organizations, and so on) interacting nonlinearly at different levels (patient, provider, organizational, policy) producing system performance and behaviours that cannot be completely understood by knowing about individual components [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR7 "Braithwaite J. Changing how we think about healthcare improvement. Br Med J. 2018;361: k2014."), [33](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR33 "Lipsitz LA. Understanding health care as a complex system: the foundation for unintended consequences. JAMA. 2012;308(3):243–4.")]. Complex adaptive systems can produce the same outcomes in numerous different ways or numerous different outcomes in the same ways. What works in one healthcare setting may not work in another; healthcare improvement is context dependent [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR34 "Coles E, Wells M, Maxwell M, Harris FM, Anderson J, Gray NM, et al. The influence of contextual factors on healthcare quality improvement initiatives: what works, for whom and in what setting? Protocol for a realist review. Syst Rev. 2017;6(1):168.")].

## Complex adaptive systems (CAS)

Complex adaptive systems (CAS) do not operate in the same way as mechanical systems [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]. Mechanical systems (1) can be decomposed and recomposed and (2) are overseen by a single entity with the authority and resources to design the system [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]. Components of mechanical systems interact linearly to produce predictable outcomes or outputs, which can be controlled by manipulating each component of the system [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR33 "Lipsitz LA. Understanding health care as a complex system: the foundation for unintended consequences. JAMA. 2012;308(3):243–4.")]. Whereas CAS are comprised of networks of components (healthcare organizations, healthcare decision-makers, healthcare professionals, healthcare providers, patients, the general public) that interact nonlinearly and produce unexpected results [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR33 "Lipsitz LA. Understanding health care as a complex system: the foundation for unintended consequences. JAMA. 2012;308(3):243–4.")]. Stakeholders with diverse interests are layered by organization, specialty and location [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]. The health system is a social system, the most complex class of systems [[36](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR36 "Kim DH. Introduction to systems thinking. 1999. 
                  https://thesystemsthinker.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/03/Introduction-to-Systems-Thinking-IMS013Epk.pdf
                  
                .")], composed of a “patterned series of interrelationships existing between individuals, groups, and institutions forming a coherent whole” [[37](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR37 "Merriam-Webster Dictionary. Social system. 2019. 
                  https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/social%20system
                  
                .")]. Regulations and policy trying to control behaviour often result in unintended consequences [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR33 "Lipsitz LA. Understanding health care as a complex system: the foundation for unintended consequences. JAMA. 2012;308(3):243–4.")].

Behaviours of independent health system agents are motivated by physical, psychological or social rules rather than system demands and needs – resulting in goals and behaviours that are likely in conflict [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]. As each agent learns over time, they modify and adapt their behaviours, leading to self-organizing units – behavioural patterns emerge rather than being designed into the system [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]. The nonlinearity of CAS results in numerous ways to produce health outcomes. Given the complexity, decomposition of complex adaptive systems may result in the loss of important contextual information critical to understanding the phenomenon under study [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")].

With increasing pressures to reduce health system inefficiencies and improve health outcomes, there is an urgency to understand where the system failures are, what causes them, what maintains system inertia (that is, behaviours, processes and so on), and what can be done to achieve health system goals. By viewing healthcare from a systems perspective and a CAS, several intrinsic system properties can be used: (1) to understand how certain decisions affect the dynamic behaviour of the system and (2) to inform implementation and decision-making [[33](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR33 "Lipsitz LA. Understanding health care as a complex system: the foundation for unintended consequences. JAMA. 2012;308(3):243–4.")].

### Box 1: defining characteristics of complex adaptive systems

Complex adaptive systems have the following characteristics (adapted from Rouse [[35](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR35 "Rouse WB. Health care as a complex adaptive system: implications for design and management. Bridge. 2008;38(1):17–25.")]):

“Nonlinear and dynamic and do not inherently reach fixed equilibrium points. As a result, system behaviours may appear to be random or chaotic”.

“Composed of independent agents whose behaviour is based on physical, psychological or social rules rather than the demands of system dynamics”.

“Because agents’ needs or desires, reflected in their rules, are not homogenous, their goals and behaviours are likely to conflict. In response to these conflicts or competitions, agents tend to adapt to each other’s behaviours”.

“Agents are intelligent. As they experiment and gain experience, agents learn and change their behaviours accordingly. Thus, overall system behaviour inherently changes over time”.

“Adaptation and learning tend to result in self-organization. Behaviour patterns emerge rather than being designed into the system. The nature of emergent behaviours may range from valuable innovations to unfortunate accidents”.

“There is no single point of control. System behaviours are often unpredictable and uncontrollable, and no one is “in charge”. Consequently, the behaviours of complex adaptive systems can usually be more easily influence than controlled”.

## Resilience engineering

High-functioning health systems will be resilient to external shocks. The health system handling of the pandemic and poor health system indicators [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12961-024-01168-w#ref-CR1 "HealthCareCAN. COVID-19 amplifies health system capacity shortfalls. 2021.

# Understanding the Consequences of Data Silos on Decision-Making and Workflow Efficiency in Healthcare Settings

![](https://www.simbo.ai/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/8171-1.jpg)

In the evolving field of healthcare, effective communication and streamlined processes are essential for ensuring quality patient care. Many healthcare organizations, however, face challenges stemming from data silos. These isolated pieces of information prevent easy data exchange and hinder effective communication. The effects of data silos can be seen throughout various healthcare settings, impacting decision-making and operational efficiency.

## The Impact of Data Silos on Patient Care

Data silos are often a result of using different information systems, which can lead to fragmented patient records. About 72% of healthcare providers in the United States face difficulties accessing complete patient data because of these incompatible systems. This issue significantly affects the quality of care. When healthcare professionals lack access to a patient’s full medical history, crucial information may be missed, raising the risk of medication errors, overlooked treatments, and unnecessary test duplications.

Healthcare professionals can waste valuable time trying to understand different data formats or contacting other departments for missing information. This communication gap slows down workflows and can delay interventions that are important for patient safety. Research shows that communication inefficiencies can cost an average 500-bed hospital more than $4 million each year, highlighting the financial impact of these issues.

#### AI Phone Agents for After-hours and Holidays

SimboConnect AI Phone Agent auto-switches to after-hours workflows during closures.

## The Cost of Inefficient Communication

The challenges brought by data silos extend beyond inconvenience. Ineffective communication in healthcare can have severe effects. Clinicians typically spend about 45 minutes each day managing communication setbacks. These delays hinder care teams’ effectiveness, increasing stress and potentially affecting patient outcomes.

The growing use of Electronic Health Records (EHRs) indicates a move towards more integrated healthcare. However, this transition has its difficulties. Although nearly 96% of non-federal acute care hospitals now use certified EHR systems, fragmentation of access still leaves many healthcare providers with incomplete patient information. This can lead to uncertainty in decision-making, misunderstandings among team members, and decreased patient satisfaction.

## Factors Contributing to Data Silos

Several factors contribute to the existence of data silos in healthcare settings. A primary cause is the use of various software solutions that are not designed to work together. Hospitals may have different systems for lab results, radiology images, and patient management, each with its own structure and protocols.

Additionally, the use of legacy systems adds complexity. High initial costs can lead organizations to stick with outdated technology that is not compatible with newer systems. As the healthcare field changes, the lack of integration becomes more apparent.

A survey among healthcare administrators and providers revealed common challenges caused by data silos, such as difficulties in obtaining lab results, imaging studies, and medication histories. These problems disrupt clinical workflows and compromise timely, effective patient care.

## Consequences on Decision-Making

In healthcare, timing is crucial. Quick and informed decisions can greatly affect patient outcomes. Data silos hinder healthcare teams’ ability to make prompt decisions. Without a complete, real-time view of patient information, there is a higher risk of missed diagnoses or inappropriate treatments.

Integrating EHRs can significantly improve patient care by allowing data to be shared seamlessly. Nevertheless, interoperability issues persist, even as the industry adopts new technologies. Various methods, including bi-directional data exchange, point-to-point integration, and API integration, can facilitate better access to patient data. However, achieving this requires a clear strategy for implementation, training, and ongoing evaluation.

Considering these challenges, healthcare organizations must prioritize EHR integration as a necessary strategy. Comprehensive access to essential data, such as lab results and medication histories, allows healthcare professionals to make informed, timely decisions for patient care.

## Workflow Efficiency and Cost Savings

The effects of data silos are evident not only in patient care but also in operational efficiency. Fragmented information creates cumbersome workflows that can reduce healthcare staff productivity. Properly integrated systems can automate administrative tasks, alleviating burdens on healthcare providers and allowing them to dedicate more time to patient care.

Organizations that have effectively implemented integrated systems have seen notable improvements. For instance, TriHealth PAC Network reported a 20% decrease in administrative tasks after integrating its systems. This enhancement improves workflow efficiency and leads to increased staff satisfaction by reducing bureaucratic barriers.

A streamlined workflow promotes quicker patient monitoring and timely interventions. Systems that provide real-time access to patient information can enhance decision-making, improve patient outcomes, and lower the risk of errors.

## Interoperability: A Critical Component

Interoperability is essential to addressing the problems caused by data silos. Effective data exchange between different healthcare systems ensures that patient information is accessible to all caregivers. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) highlights the importance of interoperability for creating a cohesive healthcare system.

Despite its benefits, healthcare organizations encounter notable challenges related to interoperability. Different systems often utilize various architectures and data formats, complicating data exchange. Organizations must actively pursue solutions that improve communication between different platforms.

In recent years, advances in health information exchange (HIE) technologies have aimed to enhance communication among healthcare providers. HIE solutions support coordinated care by enabling secure sharing of patient data across networks. These tools can help address issues stemming from data silos and improve transitions in care across different settings.

## The Role of AI and Workflow Automation

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and workflow automation play important roles in addressing data silo challenges in healthcare. By automating routine tasks, AI can improve operational efficiency, allowing human resources to focus on critical aspects of patient care. For medical practice administrators and IT managers, incorporating AI solutions can be transformative.

AI-driven tools can streamline scheduling, patient intake, and health record management, reducing time spent on administrative duties. Automation aids in consolidating data from different sources, allowing healthcare professionals quick access to complete patient information.

For example, AI can identify discrepancies or missing records during patient intake, alerting staff before problems escalate. This capability enhances data accuracy and supports timely decision-making based on reliable information.

Automated patient interactions, such as appointment reminders, can improve patient engagement and lessen staff workload. As healthcare organizations embrace telehealth and virtual care, AI and workflow automation will be key in managing these interactions effectively.

## Challenges and Considerations

However, integrating AI and automation into healthcare workflows presents challenges. Data security and privacy concerns are significant. With increasing amounts of data being collected, the risk of data breaches grows. Organizations must ensure their systems are secure and capable of protecting sensitive patient information as required by regulations like HIPAA.

Employee resistance can also hinder the adoption of new technologies. Change management strategies are essential for easing transitions and securing staff support. Administrators must help employees understand the benefits of new technologies and provide adequate training.

Healthcare organizations should also remain adaptable as the field evolves. As technology advances, solutions must be scalable to address the changing needs of patient care and compliance.

## Comprehensive Strategies for Improvement

To effectively address the issues stemming from data silos, healthcare organizations should create comprehensive strategies focusing on three main components: integration, training, and evaluation.

The healthcare environment requires medical practice administrators and IT managers to develop systems that encourage collaboration and communication. By addressing the challenges of data silos and integrating solutions like AI and workflow automation, healthcare organizations can enhance decision-making, improve patient outcomes, and increase overall efficiency in care delivery.

As the demand for efficient healthcare continues to grow, it is essential for healthcare leaders to adopt an integrated approach that prioritizes patient care in the United States. By recognizing the impact of data silos and tackling them directly, healthcare organizations can create a more efficient, patient-focused future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Siloed health strategies impede collaboration, leading to fragmented care and inefficient resource use, ultimately affecting patient outcomes and increasing healthcare costs.
Collaborative frameworks enhance communication, streamline processes, and integrate services, resulting in improved patient care, reduced redundancies, and increased operational efficiency.
Successful integration examples include health information exchanges and multidisciplinary care teams, which enhance data sharing and patient-centered care, demonstrating the One Health approach.
